narrative:
Ashcombe Advisory Partners is a corporate communications and investor
relations advisory founded in 2014, working with mid-market public and
pre-IPO companies on positioning, announcements, and reputation. Its work is
correspondence: the firm lives in its clients' inboxes, drafting messaging,
routing approvals, and managing announcement timelines by email, so the paper
trail is dominated by threads rather than formal deliverables.

The firm runs lean. A managing partner leads a bench of advisors and analysts
who each carry two or three client relationships at once, supported by a small
operations team that keeps schedules, files, and the mailbox in order. Client
work is organized as discrete engagements, each opening with a signed letter
and a kickoff memo and then running mostly over email: status threads, quick
approvals, and the occasional transmittal of a working document. Internal
mail is mundane and constant, the ordinary logistics of a busy small office.

Advisors write plainly and quickly. A thread opens with a clear subject, and
replies stay short and answer the message before them. Formal documents
(engagement letters, the periodic firm overview, financial summaries) are
sparse by design; the firm's character is in how it corresponds, not in how
much it writes down.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0001",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- If a doc carries `engagement_position`, honor it: it states where the
  document sits in its engagement's timeline. Do not describe progress or a
  phase inconsistent with it, and do not state the engagement's start or
  end date (those are fact placeholders, not yours to write).
- If a doc carries `firm_digest`, it is the ONLY firm history you may treat
  as established as of this document's date. Reference clients only through
  the placeholders it names; claim no engagement it does not list; present
  nothing later than this document as already true.
- If a doc carries `reporting_line`, the person reports to exactly who it
  names and to no one else. Do not name any other person or title as their
  supervisor; a reporting relationship is owned by the ledger, not invented.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Engagement Letter: Corporate Positioning for Barrera Group

authors.name: Julie Hill
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Leads the firm and sets its plain-spoken house style, insisting that anything going to a client answer the question in the first line. Her own email is short and decisive, quick to name who owns the next move and by when. She reads the whole thread before she replies and dislikes messaging that hedges when the moment calls for a clear position.

participants.name: Daniel Valenzuela
participants.title: Advisor
participants.persona: An advisor who runs two or three engagements at once and writes to keep each one moving, favoring short status threads that end with a specific ask. His voice is practical and even, more interested in getting an approval routed than in polishing the sentence. He is good at translating a client's worry into a concrete next step.

participants.name: Michelle Rose
participants.title: Communications Associate
participants.persona: A communications associate who assembles drafts and routes them for approval, writing in a warm, readable style that makes a dense update approachable. She organizes a note around what changed and what is left, and is quick to put a small question in a short email rather than let it stall. Her threads stay friendly without wandering off the point.

docs.engagement_summary: Corporate Positioning for Barrera Group, running about 5 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.

facts.hint: amount of money; place where the figure belongs
calendar date; place where the date belongs
proper name; place where the name belongs

docs.guidance: Formal engagement letter on firm letterhead confirming the engagement. Do NOT repeat the firm's name as a heading or title line: the letterhead already carries it. Open with the client's inside address and a salutation, then cover scope and approach, the team, and the fee and start date, followed by the standard terms a real engagement letter carries -- each as its own short headed paragraph: fees and payment, term and termination, confidentiality, limitation of liability, and governing law. Close with a signature request and end with a sigblock block signed by the letter's author and the client contact.
docs.engagement_position: This document is dated about 0% of the way through the engagement (at or before kickoff); it runs roughly 5 months end to end. Do not describe progress inconsistent with that position, and do not state the engagement's start or end date.

participants.name: Lisa Flores
participants.title: Director of Operations
participants.persona: Runs operations for the firm and writes in a steady, procedural tone that documents what was agreed and who owns it. She favors complete sentences and a clean paper trail, and tends to restate a commitment so it cannot be misread later. Her notes are polite, thorough, and built to keep the small office running on schedule.

participants.name: Pamela Rojas
participants.title: Office Manager
participants.persona: An office manager who keeps schedules, files, and the mailbox in order and writes in a friendly, direct register aimed at getting a date or a detail confirmed. Her email is brief and logistics-first, with little narrative and no wasted words. She is the one who turns a loose plan into a booked, written commitment.

docs.guidance: Plain-text status email within the engagement thread: a greeting line naming the recipients, 2-3 short paragraphs of progress and asks, a sign-off line with the sender's name. The subject lives in the message header, not the body. No sigblock. This opens the thread: set the topic up clearly for the replies that follow. Your signature is appended automatically; do not type one.
docs.engagement_position: This document is dated about 19% of the way through the engagement (in the early phase); it runs roughly 5 months end to end. Do not describe progress inconsistent with that position, and do not state the engagement's start or end date.
